transcript.text: Drop the notes into the chat.
This meeting is being recorded.
How's everyone's week going?
We're good.
Yeah.
What did we do this week?
A little webinar.
My in-laws are here, so it's been helpful for kids.
How about you?
Good, good, busy.
Everything's crammed before holiday break.
Do you guys usually have like the last two weeks people are off?
I think it's kind of like definitely the last week.
I think it bleeds into obviously the first week of January, but definitely the last week is off.
So you're still here tomorrow, or sorry, next week, the week after you're gone.
Then January, it depends.
Yeah.
You guys doing anything for the holidays?
Staying put, which is good.
Same.
I'm, uh, yeah, I think I'm doing the same.
Or we might go to San Diego, just like we're in Oakland.
It's not too far of a drive.
Hey, Raymond.
Hello.
How's it going?
I saw you join the team recently on the DevRel side.
That was your first weeks at Webflow, Or is it your first weeks?
It's, uh, about four weeks or so, so first month.
Cool.
I saw your, or Liz showed me your latest, uh, your blog post on Korean extension.
like the cat pictures.
Glad you liked that.
think everybody in this room already knew that stuff, but it was all new to me and it was all fun to learn.
I think that storytelling is, like, really good.
I mean, I also run marketing for, like, us, and, like, it's kind of our formula.
It's like, we just, like, we do all this cool stuff for you guys with our other clients, and then we just kind of talk about it in as genuine a way as possible.
You guys know if Colin's joining?
I guess I was waiting for him before kicking things off, but I'm happy to give him another minute or two.
And there he is, speaking of the devil.
Cool.
We got a lot of folks here today.
Raymond, glad you joined.
We do have, like, some stuff to share on the use case guides that you've been reviewing.
So we can have, like, chat about that.
Hey, Colin, how's it going?
Cool.
Okay.
I will get started.
So, today, we're going to go through...
through...

Google integrations, we have quite a bit to talk about on the use case guides, there's a lot of feedback and I think we're trying to kind of like generalize it and try to see how we can just improve how we write and then there are some pieces of feedback that I think makes sense to discuss, you know, like for example, we made kind of a decision early on to like try not to have code snippets because, you know, like you got to update them and pointing to the canonical source seemed easier in some sense and also like useful in some sense, I think for some articles, you know, when you flagged that like maybe it makes sense to have some kind of simple instructions to just discuss stuff like that.
There's a category refresh that Liz has been working on.
And what else?
have the developer hub, which we don't have anything this week, but we're just going to give an update of how we're thinking about it.
And then we're getting a lot of folks, not a lot, but like an increasing number of people like asking about the integration pages.
And we want to put in a process that, you know, you can get people to get feedback and, you know, somebody, whether it's us or you guys, has the ability to go and update.
So that's the day.
I'll start with like a work stream update and like some things that are coming up since we're getting close to the end of the year.
So in recent weeks, I just want to flag like, you know, some of the stuff outside of like normal kind of content work streams we're doing.
So the code components, Ed and Liz and I kind of drove, there's been quite a bit on the integration pages, getting the use case guides out the door.
And then
And we have some plans for, like, what a V2 for the integration pages look like.
A change that we're going to make coming up, like, as we get into next year is we started hiring a lot more experts.
And we want to go to a model where, like, depending on the project, Liz, who's kind of the constant here, pulls in the right person.
So, I mean, I think, you know, I've kind of held it down as a journalist, but we hired somebody much longer than I have.
We hired somebody, actually, hopefully you guys are happy with that.
Just like, again, super deep developer marketing, their whole career here essentially came from GitHub, Sentry.
This you might not recognize, but it's like a kind of AP testing tool, kind of like LaunchDarkly.
And I know you guys are kind of building that in with the guys' company that you guys acquired.
We have Kirk.

Colin, who's like on just like AI content workflows, he gets super deep there, his background's like, he actually used to be an SEO person, and then at some point he saw the writing on the wall with AI, and just like completely retrained himself as an engineer, so he's kind of a unicorn that helps us build workflows with good taste.

And then I'm here to support as like a growth journalist, I mean, honestly, I don't know what to call my name, and how that'll look is, so the integration pages, trying to bring in, Liz and I trying to bring in Kathy and Kirkland, these case guides, like a lot of the feedback, you know, again, trying to bring in Kathy and Kirkland, like, just be a lot faster with making the workflows better.
Code components, guess, question mark, but like, if we were to work on that, I think that's probably something I'll stay close to, and then, you know, I think the last QBR we had was...
Like, mid-October, so I guess we're roughly planning for, like, sometime in January to, like, review this last quarter and think a little bit about what to work on next.
And the last thing to note, we typically have these last two weeks as quiet weeks.
We don't have this meeting.
We'll still kind of work on things and update you async.
And depending on the date, like, some of us are still here.
But before you join, Colin, was talking to Luke, and it seems like that line-slip of vacations.
you wanted to check in in that time period, I will be home doing nothing, so I can hang out.
But it's not required at all.
I want Webflow to be available during that time period.
Okay.
Well, I guess depending on the combo today and next week, it seems like what we're doing is still fairly structured, and there's not a lot of questions, but, yeah, I guess we'll get into it.
Um.
Cool, I'll hand it over to you, Liz, if you can go through the sections here.
Muted.
So for integrations, basically, we're focusing a lot on what that next step is in terms of optimization.
We have, at least in our list, about 200 left, so with 30 a week, we're going to run out very soon.
We're wanting to think what the next steps are, and then I just did a quick little analysis of SEO performance on top-performing pages.
So these would obviously be, like, what we'd want to tackle first, and then move down that list.
And this will also help us inform the category refreshes and, like, what we might want to feature or push up for top-performing traffic.
So it's just, like, a little add-on to the existing performance.
There's a lot of directions we can go with this, and so I made a list of them, and, you know, we can, like, engage with them at varying degrees.
So not just...
I'm the managing editor.
He's also doing a lot of work on, like, what that proposal looks like.

And so the more obvious things are, of course, thinking about CRO practices and what that means for Webflow around integration pages and how much we'd want to actually invest in that or what kind of net positive that would mean for y'all, what we can do to improve positioning, detailed FAQs.
So trying to get those a bit better, a bit stronger for search.
Yeah.
And I think that's about it.
So, like, Najum's working to continue to own end 10 production, but simultaneously building out the strategy so that we can very quickly start pivoting to the optimization phase.
Any thoughts about this?
I don't have a question on, yeah, I guess, which ones are above the fold?
Well, I mean, which ones are on page one, which ones are top of fold?
If we have that top 10 list, then I assume we can get the full list.
I mean, you don't need to share with me right now, but just generally, it's interesting which ones we are top ranking for and which ones are making a position.
I'd love to see the competition on all these pages if there was a domain to look into that.
Yeah, we can pull that together.
I will say, like, I think, did we get this from Search Console, Liz?
Yes, yes.
So they'd, like, do this.
So, for example, like, Google Tag Manager.
I think the interesting thing is, like, we definitely need to, like, unpack it.
So the reason that says 12 is, like, I'm mostly certain it ranks number one for Webflow Google Tag Manager integration, but, like, because it's ranking for a bunch of other keywords, I imagine.
so it's averaging outcast everything.
Oh, okay.
Yeah, so I think it makes sense to, like, unpack all of it.

It's like, hey, you know, you're probably page one, I'm going to assume, for almost all of the, like, Webflow integration ones, and then for some of the, like, non-branded, organic, like, people just Googling, you know, Google Tag Manager integration.
That's exactly what I was wondering.
Seeing, like, a Calendly Webflow position.
16 feels odd.
And then so I was wondering like, oh, is that really like a thing that people are talking about in the space?
So, okay, helpful.
I appreciate you understanding how that number was gathered.
Yeah, we can do a more detailed build out of that and then feed that back into the strategy because this is kind of like a new world for us in terms of integration pages.
Cool.
Use case guides.

So, for Raymond mostly, I started working on hoping we can have also like another call if we don't get through it all today, but more of a conversation around what sort of quality and expectations we'd want to produce on these.

The first ones have been actioned and are being staged, but we could, if we want, if we think it's a good use of time, we could go through and talk about these a bit more.
But I think there's a little bit of...
Codify needed on our end.
And then I also did have a question for any Webflow docs.
They're all public facing, right?
Like this is all accessible on the internet.
Yes.
Okay.
All right.

Because our research agent is building out that assignment details, but we're testing some other workflows and it's like with those workflows, having that front load thinking with a single source of truth is usually a lot stronger.

And so I'm thinking if there's like an opportunity for us to build that in and kind of move away from the research agent doing that work, we could look into that if we're seeing some like validation issues on the output.
What would you imagine the signals or the truth to be in these scenarios?
So literally just one single doc, it's basically like a rag system.
So we would just look at that single doc on that integration or whatever, instead of letting our agent make a decision about what's on the internet.
That's it's that simple.
But at scale that.
That could mean even just downloading those webpages and using them and not allowing for that crawl step.
Totally.
These integrations, are they typically just one Webflow and one external source?
I know the Stripe one involved Cloudflare also, and we were looking at a membership site with a Cloudflare or a Firebase poll.
Are we thinking about a world where we would just effectively rag Webflow devdocs, rag Stripe devdocs, and rag Cloudflare and see how we could put it all together?
I mean, we could.
It's obviously a bit more of a lift, but the quality would be much higher.
I'm trying to think back to how we actually made this as well with Kirkland.
I think we already do that.
I think I've read through a few of Raymond's comments and some of the, like, finer details.
I've
I think we almost need to go even deeper.
One of the problems just off the top of my head is if we limit to Webflow.com, you still get a lot of noise.
You have help docs, forum stuff, universities.
A lot of times they talk about roughly the same topic and they're not totally in sync.
And I think the AI just has trouble figuring it out and recommending the best approach.
dream state would be just falling from the developer's subdomain.
And because a lot of Webflow content will be for no-code implementations.
And if this is all expected to be code implementations or our apps, but in these examples, we're going for code implementations.
I would hope that our dev docs and our guides in our dev docs are sufficient enough to guide an AI through how to do our best practice for implementation.
I don't know if that would be true for the external party that we would be wanting to offer.
Yeah, I feel like the answer is, like, I guess it depends based on what the other integration is.
But I guess to kind of reiterate what Liz was saying initially, like, got a bunch of comments from Raymond.
I think we're trying to, like, almost generalize them a little bit into themes so we can figure out how to tackle all of them.

Because right now what we're doing is, like, we're just kind of addressing them one by one, which still works, but kind of slows things down, you know, and also makes Raymond's life a little bit more.
They're, you know, cumbersome because you've got to keep reviewing them.
And then, so that's kind of what doing right now.
And then what we're trying to do is, like, yeah, generalize.
And then, okay, it's like, how do we solve this kind of at the scale so you don't have to keep reviewing, basically.
Is that accurate, Liz?
Mm-hmm.
Yeah.
I don't know if you have any, like, feedback for us right now, Raymond, in call.
Or, like, priorities you'd like us to prioritize.
Mm-hmm.
And I, so I think one of the things that that hit me is that when I first started...
Looking at these, I kind of went into it with the expectation of a technical blog post, which is perhaps not what the intended audience was.

So I expected to see a bit more code than was there, and I don't think we need to turn them into technical blog posts that would greatly expand the amount of work needed to even build a simple proof of concept of all these integrations.
It would take a lot more time.

I do think, though, that since so much is you're using this third-party X, and X has data in this shape, and Webflow has this shape, I think seeing something at least, like here's an example of SoundCloud's data, and this is the typical Webflow content item, this is how you would need to transform that in order to sync up the connection between them.
It doesn't have to be real code.
It could just be, like, here's the JSON from them.
Here's what we expect.
And as a
As I get an idea of what kind of work that is.
I don't have to see you actually transforming it by hand, if that makes sense.
Yeah.

I think that was the level we were trying to aim at, where it's like, hey, if you read this, Colin, me if I'm wrong, if you read this, you would have a roughly good idea of how much work it would take and roughly what you would have to do, but you wouldn't be able to totally just follow it and get it done.
But we will link you to those places.
That was the intention.
That was the intention of these.
So while the integration hub core is saying what is possible for inspiration of integration, these guides would be pseudo samples of here is a thing you can do and here is how you would do it.
And the feedback that Ray is giving is a bit more pseudo code would help people understand the shape that they would need to take to approach it.
Not saying produce a whole app.
That's the other screen that we've been talking about.
That makes sense.
And I think even that pattern is super useful that the shape of data from the integration, shape of data, Webflow, they don't totally match.
And that could be, I don't know, we'll have to look at it on our site.
But that is something you could kind of split out separately and make sure we do that right.
Because we know that exists in a lot of these.
One more thing I'd probably mention, and I didn't mention this in my comments, but pretty much every single integration app talks about rate limits and testing and good housekeeping.
And yes, that's good, like brush your teeth every day.
But it gets repetitive and it's also applicable to every single technical article on the Internet.
So I would almost suggest not worrying about that as much.
Because it is expected in anything you build at all.
Like you're going to test.
You're going to have fallback in case the APIs are broken.
know, it's something that could actually, you know, if I'm asking you to add more content, that could outweigh it.
That could reduce some as well, if that makes sense.
Yep.
I think just zooming out a little bit, like, if there are, like, just the way that feedback you frame right now is, like, actually extremely helpful, like, just how you generalized it.
It's, like, if you have things like that, shoot them our way.
That one is, like, super easy to very quickly address.
Yeah.
So that makes sense.
I guess from my side, like, we have a workflow is generating the kind of format you're seeing right now.
How we want it to work for the integration pages is, like, we get to the part where you guys are, like, cool.
I mean, it's not totally perfect, but let's get out the door and learn.
So with integration pages, I think.
That's working extremely well.
And now we have signals where, you know, Liz is showing you guys, like, here are the ones we go back and kind of prioritize a little bit more effort on.
You know, maybe we add a few screenshots, maybe we do this or that.
I don't know if I totally know what that bar is for use case guides.
So I don't know if Raymond, that's, or Colin, that's something we can figure out where it's like, cool, shape of data not matching, having a section for that seems helpful.
Is that required to get it published so we can get signal?
Or is that something we need to get done before?

I would, so if our core value is, if the core goal of these guides is to provide value, both for agentic searches and for actual organic people exploring, because the whole genesis was people reading this and saying, oh, I actually want to do that.
How would I do that?
Then I would say to the point that this actually can give someone confidence.
In of how they would do the implementation, we should aim for what that bar looks like.
And if Ray is saying he would want to at least know kind of what the general shape is of how he would approach integration, I would say let's say that's the floor.
But at that point, I'm open to experimentation.
I'm not expecting these to be massively clicked on.
These are going to be, can this help guide our sales engineering team?
Can this help?
Will this get agentic search traffic?
And so I don't know what metrics we'll track on this one, really, at this point.
But it's going to be just general, in my mind, anecdotal feedback for the next couple of months as we kick this off.
Okay, that sounds good.
So I guess maybe the ask for you, Raymond, is like if you give feedback or even generalized kind of like themes of things that we could do better.
If you could specify also like this is a kind of V2 that we can add later and still publish or this is something we definitely need to get right before.
Kind of this content, you know, sees the light of day on the, on the web.
Yeah, right, right now, like, it's good for, trying to phrase this properly, right now the content is, is great if, you know, not much about Webflow, that it has APIs, for example.
So, seeing that you can integrate with SoundCloud, that sparks, right?
Like, oh, now I'm more interested.
That's already, the problem is, that the docs we're talking about right now are one step below that.
We already, we already have those upstream.
That's the integration hub as it stands.
So these are supposed to be a little more in depth of giving people understanding of, like, a thing you could do.
Not just, this is an integration with SoundCloud, this is integration with Notion.
This is a, here's a, here's a thing you could do with that integration.
Then I think it, I think it gets a bit more before it goes live.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But I, I think we're, I think we're very close, Jason.
Okay.
Cool.
Yeah.
So I guess from our side, I'm sorry.
Yeah.
I think the next step is clear.
Like these high level expectations are pretty clear.

Just take this back to the content engine, kind of pause, like just producing it and see if we can get the baseline correct and then kind of move forward from there, but maybe a little bit work with engineering just to, because the needs here are a bit different than integration pages, you know.

I would say also, if we could just like, like kind of, again, spent, you know, quite a bit of time adding all the comments, if we could just group them into themes, I think that just visually is a little bit more helpful, because I think there will maybe be things that are a little heavier lift that we could kind of punt to a V2.
I think if we could do that, I could get on the same page a little quicker.
I don't if everyone has time to like, review all the articles.
Like, all the articles.
you.
Could we do that for Colin?
Yep.
Yeah, yeah.
And calibration probably too.
Simultaneously.
Oh, so just a quick category refresh.
We're ready to go on this.

And we do have access to the CMS to make these changes.
I'm just looking for a green light or if you still, Colin, wanted to put this through the partnerships team.
I've got no feedback, so I'm comfortable just pushing.
Is Luke still on?
Yes.
Yep.
Yeah, what we're talking about right now is, as Greg has been mentioning in the internal pages, changing the categories.
This should not be a significant hit to SEO, but I just want to make sure you're aware.
We would be changing the categories and reorganizing which pages fall under which categories, which would mean the subcategory URLs would be changed.
So it would change from an integration type x, integration hyphen, integration type slash x would change to integration type slash assets, anti-spam, whatever it needs to be.

So we would be taking down some potential category pages, and I would love to take something back to Michael and Vivian on if there's a way to 301 those so that we don't just drop that SEO on the table.
Yeah, mean, there should be a redirect for any URL changes that we create.
Yep, I just want to make sure that we're setting that up on our side for changing these.
Yeah, I think Vivian would be the person that would help you do all of that.
Okay, cool.
I'm with Tiger in.
So, Liz, my answer to that is give us Dave.
Here to ask Vivian how we would execute on the 301 plan.
Okay.
I don't know if we have that.
Zach, do we have that flexibility through API on Webflow-owned sites?
There should be an API.
There's an API for it.
I don't know if we can use it.
Could you drop the workspace slug in the Zoom chat and I can try and confirm.
Okay.
It's brand workspace.
I'll send it to you, though.
Okay.
Yeah, we can follow up with that.
So, let's give us a couple days to figure out what the plan is for 301, but I'm okay with the categories as proposed.
Okay.
Cool.
So, update intake process.
This is still, like, on my plate to set that up.
I know we had one last week.
I think we have still, like, a little bit of time, but as we move into more, you know, content updating, content updating, refreshes.
Optimization, definitely want to establish something like this and open to ideas or feedback.
then the last bit, Developer Hub, guess I can hand this over to Jason because he's a lot more excited about this than me for an update.
Oh, for the developer hub?
Yeah, so I think we just need a little bit of time.

Just like, yeah, just something a little bit more cohesive of like, hey, here's what developers could look like.
Is that aligned with like how you're thinking about it, Colin?
Yeah.
do you think about maybe a little bit more scope down?

appreciate it.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Sync on content strategy, use case guides, and integration pages.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=235.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Use Case Guides Paused:** Production is paused to implement feedback, primarily adding JSON examples to show data transformation between Webflow and integrations. This is a pre-publication requirement to make the guides actionable.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1016.0)
  - [**Integration Page Strategy:** With production nearly complete, the focus shifts to optimization. The initial plan is to analyze SEO performance (using unpacked Search Console data) to prioritize pages for CRO and content improvements.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=553.0)
  - [**Category Refresh Blocked:** The planned refresh is blocked pending a 301 redirect strategy. This is critical to preserve SEO value from old URLs, which will be lost otherwise.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1546.0)
  - [**Developer Hub Vision:** GrowthX will draft a vision for a cohesive developer hub, integrating content streams like the blog and code components into a single destination.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1684.0)

## Topics

### Use Case Guides: Feedback & Strategy

  - [**Problem:** Current guides lack the technical detail needed to be actionable for developers.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1016.0)
  - [**Solution:** Add JSON examples showing data transformation between an integration's API and Webflow's CMS.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1047.0)
      - [**Rationale:** This provides a concrete understanding of the work involved without requiring full code snippets.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1089.0)
  - [**Content Scope:** Remove repetitive, generic advice (e.g., testing, rate limits) to make room for more valuable technical content.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1167.0)
  - [**Publication Bar:** The new JSON examples are a pre-publication requirement.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1288.0)
      - [**Rationale:** The guides must provide confidence in the implementation process to be valuable.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1296.0)
      - [**Metrics:** Success will be measured anecdotally (e.g., sales engineering feedback), not by traffic.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1338.0)
  - [**Workflow Improvement:** Explore using a RAG system with a single source of truth (e.g., Webflow's `developer.webflow.com` subdomain) to improve AI-generated content quality.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)

### Integration Pages: Optimization Strategy

  - [**Context:** With \~200 pages left to produce, the focus shifts from production to optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=553.0)
  - [**Initial Analysis:** A review of top-performing pages (via Search Console) will inform optimization priorities.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=566.0)
      - [**Clarification:** The average position metric (e.g., "12" for Google Tag Manager) is misleading. It averages performance across all keywords, including non-branded ones where the page may rank lower.](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=675.0)
      - [**Action:** Unpack this data to see performance for specific keywords (e.g., "Webflow Google Tag Manager integration" vs. "Google Tag Manager integration").](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=683.0)
  - [**Optimization Tactics:**](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=589.0)
      - [CRO best practices](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=589.0)

### Category Refresh: Blocked

  - [**Plan:** Reorganize integration categories and update URLs (e.g., `.../integration-type/x` → `.../assets/x`).](https://fathom.video/share/Q4d1mqZBo9ygVWh1sZdCme1_nxcVMJq5?tab=summary&timestamp=1546.0)

### Developer Hub: Vision
